title: Unsupervised Learning
description: Clustering, dimensionality reduction, and anomaly detection: algorithms that find hidden structure in unlabeled data with FAANG-depth intuition.
details: Unsupervised learning discovers patterns in data without labels. This file covers clustering (K-Means, DBSCAN, hierarchical, GMM), dimensionality reduction (PCA, t-SNE, UMAP), and anomaly detection (Isolation Forest, LOF, One-Class SVM). Each section covers the algorithm, mathematical foundation, hyperparameter tuning, and practical FAANG interview questions. Key themes: the tradeoff between scalability and cluster shape flexibility, the importance of feature scaling, and the distinction between global and local structure preservation in embeddings.

sections.title: K-Means Clustering
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Partition n points into k clusters where each point belongs to the nearest centroid. Lloyd's algorithm alternates between assignment (points→centroid) and update (centroid→mean of assigned points).

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete: K-Means on 1M points with k=10 converges in ~30 iterations, ~200ms on a single CPU core. k-means++ initialization adds ~5ms but reduces iterations by 2–3× vs random init.

Algorithm:

  • Initialization: k-means++ selects k initial centroids that are far apart (smart init)
  • Assign: Each point goes to nearest centroid (Euclidean distance)
  • Update: Recompute centroid as mean of assigned points
  • Repeat until convergence or max iterations

Choosing k: Elbow method (WCSS vs k), silhouette score (higher = better separation), gap statistic. Limitations: K-Means assumes spherical clusters of equal size — fails on elongated or varying density clusters.
sections.codeBlock:
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
from sklearn.metrics import silhouette_score
import numpy as np

# K-Means with k-means++ initialization
kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=5, init='k-means++',
                n_init=10, random_state=42)
labels = kmeans.fit_predict(X)

# Elbow method
inertias = []
for k in range(2, 11):
    kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=k, init='k-means++',
                    n_init=10, random_state=42)
    kmeans.fit(X)
    inertias.append(kmeans.inertia_)
    sil = silhouette_score(X, kmeans.labels_)
    print(f'k={k}: inertia={kmeans.inertia_:.1f}, sil={sil:.3f}')

# Get centroids and labels
centroids = kmeans.cluster_centers_
print(f'Cluster sizes: {np.bincount(kmeans.labels_)}')

sections.title: DBSCAN
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Density-based clustering — find high-density regions separated by low-density regions. Points in sparse regions are labeled as noise. No need to specify k!

Parameters:

  • eps (epsilon): Radius for neighborhood. Too small → many noise points. Too large → everything merges.
  • minPts: Minimum points to form a dense region. Larger → smoother clusters. Default: 2·n_features (rule of thumb).

Point types: Core (≥minPts in eps-radius), Border (< minPts but has core neighbor), Noise (neither). HDBSCAN extends DBSCAN by making eps adaptive across densities.

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete: DBSCAN on 100K points with eps=0.5 runs in ~50ms (KD-tree index). HDBSCAN on the same data takes ~200ms but produces better clusters across varying densities. eps too small → 50%+ labeled as noise; too large → one giant cluster. Use k-distance graph elbow to set eps.
sections.codeBlock:
from sklearn.cluster import DBSCAN, HDBSCAN
from sklearn.neighbors import NearestNeighbors

# Find optimal eps: k-distance graph
nn = NearestNeighbors(n_neighbors=5)
distances, _ = nn.fit_transform(X)
distances = np.sort(distances[:, -1])
# Plot: look for the 'elbow' point

# DBSCAN
db = DBSCAN(eps=0.5, min_samples=5, metric='euclidean')
labels = db.fit_predict(X)
n_clusters = len(set(labels)) - (1 if -1 in labels else 0)
n_noise = list(labels).count(-1)
print(f'Clusters: {n_clusters}, Noise: {n_noise}')

# HDBSCAN (adaptive eps)
hdb = HDBSCAN(min_cluster_size=5, min_samples=5)
labels = hdb.fit_predict(X)

sections.title: Hierarchical Clustering
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Build a tree of clusters — either bottom-up (agglomerative: start with each point as its own cluster, merge closest pair) or top-down (divisive: start with one cluster, split recursively).

Linkage methods:

  • Single: min distance between clusters — chaining effect, elongated clusters
  • Complete: max distance — compact clusters, sensitive to outliers
  • Average: mean distance — balance between single and complete
  • Ward: minimize within-cluster variance — produces compact clusters, similar to K-Means

[Warning] Complexity: O(n³) naive, O(n²) with priority queue. Not suitable for > 10K points. Use dendrogram to choose cutoff height → determines number of clusters.

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete: Agglomerative clustering with Ward linkage on 10K points: O(n³) naive = 10¹² operations → ~30s. With SLINK/CLINK (O(n²)), same data → ~200ms. For >50K points, use HDBSCAN or mini-batch K-Means instead. Dendrogram cutoff at height=0.3 gives ~5 clusters in most real datasets.
sections.codeBlock:
from scipy.cluster.hierarchy import dendrogram, linkage
from sklearn.cluster import AgglomerativeClustering
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Hierarchical clustering
Z = linkage(X, method='ward')  # linkage matrix

# Plot dendrogram
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))
dendrogram(Z, truncate_mode='level', p=3)
plt.axhline(y=cutoff_height, color='red', linestyle='--')
plt.show()

# Agglomerative with k clusters
model = AgglomerativeClustering(
    n_clusters=5,
    linkage='ward',
    metric='euclidean'
)
labels = model.fit_predict(X)

sections.title: Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM) & EM
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Soft clustering — each point belongs to every cluster with a probability. GMM assumes data is generated by a mixture of k Gaussian distributions, each with its own mean (μ), covariance (Σ), and mixing weight (π).

EM Algorithm:

  • E-step: Compute responsibility γᵢₖ = P(zₖ|xᵢ, θ) — probability that point i belongs to component k
  • M-step: Update parameters using weighted MLE: μₖ = Σγᵢₖ·xᵢ / Σγᵢₖ, Σₖ = Σγᵢₖ·(xᵢ-μₖ)(xᵢ-μₖ)ᵀ / Σγᵢₖ

Covariance types: 'full' (each component has its own covariance ellipse), 'tied' (shared covariance), 'diag' (axis-aligned ellipses), 'spherical' (circles). Use BIC/AIC to select component count and covariance type.

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete: GMM with full covariance on 100K points × 10D with k=5: EM converges in ~50 iterations, ~500ms. Diag covariance: ~100ms (5× faster). BIC for k=3→10: typically elbow at k=4-6. Full covariance has O(k·d²) params vs diag O(k·d) — for d=100, full cov = 50K params/component, diag = 100.
sections.codeBlock:
from sklearn.mixture import GaussianMixture
import numpy as np

# GMM with full covariance
model = GaussianMixture(
    n_components=3,
    covariance_type='full',   # 'tied', 'diag', 'spherical'
    random_state=42,
    n_init=5
)
model.fit(X)
labels = model.predict(X)           # hard assignment
probas = model.predict_proba(X)     # soft assignment P(z|x)
print(f'Weights: {model.weights_}')
print(f'Means: {model.means_}')
print(f'BIC: {model.bic(X):.1f}')

# Model selection via BIC
bics = []
for k in range(1, 10):
    gmm = GaussianMixture(n_components=k, covariance_type='full')
    gmm.fit(X)
    bics.append(gmm.bic(X))
best_k = np.argmin(bics) + 1
print(f'Best k by BIC: {best_k}')

sections.title: Principal Component Analysis (PCA)
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Find the directions of maximum variance in the data. PCA rotates the coordinate system so that the first axis captures the most variance, the second captures the next most (orthogonal), and so on.

Connections:

  • Eigenvalue decomposition: Cov(X) = (1/n)·XᵀX. Eigenvectors = principal components, eigenvalues = variance explained by each component.
  • SVD: X = UΣVᵀ. Columns of V are principal components. UΣ are the projected data.
  • Explained variance ratio: λᵢ/Σλⱼ — choose k where cumulative ≥ 0.95 (95% rule)

[Practical Workflow] Workflow: Always standardize before PCA (features with larger scales dominate). Use scree plot to decide k. PCA assumes linear relationships — use kernel PCA or autoencoders for non-linear.

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete: PCA on 10K×1K matrix takes ~50ms via randomized SVD (n_components=50). Full SVD: ~500ms. Standardization adds ~5ms. Explained variance: first 50 components typically capture 80-95% of variance in real datasets. Reconstruction error (MSE) with 50 components: ~0.05 on normalized data.
sections.codeBlock:
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
import numpy as np

# Standardize first
X_scaled = StandardScaler().fit_transform(X)

# PCA
pca = PCA(n_components=0.95)  # keep 95% variance
X_pca = pca.fit_transform(X_scaled)

print(f'Original dims: {X.shape[1]}')
print(f'Reduced dims: {X_pca.shape[1]}')
print(f'Explained variance ratio: {pca.explained_variance_ratio_}')
print(f'Cumulative: {np.cumsum(pca.explained_variance_ratio_)}')

# Via SVD (what PCA does internally)
U, S, Vt = np.linalg.svd(X_scaled, full_matrices=False)
components = Vt.T  # principal components
projected = U @ np.diag(S)  # same as X_pca

# Reconstruction (denoising)
X_reconstructed = pca.inverse_transform(X_pca)

sections.title: t-SNE & UMAP
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Both are visualization tools that preserve neighborhood structure. t-SNE optimizes KL divergence between high-dim and low-dim similarity distributions. UMAP uses cross-entropy and is faster/scales better.

Critical caveat: For visualization ONLY. Non-deterministic, no inverse transform, distances in the embedding are not meaningful. t-SNE clusters can be artifacts of the perplexity hyperparameter.

Key differences:

  • t-SNE: Preserves local structure (neighbors stay neighbors). Perplexity ≈ 30 (default). Slow on > 10K points. Use PCA first to reduce to 50 dimensions as preprocessing.
  • UMAP: Preserves both local AND global structure. Much faster (N log N vs N²). Better for visualizing global cluster relationships. Can transform new data (transform method).

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete: t-SNE on 10K points × 50D takes ~3 minutes (O(N²) ≈ 100M pairwise distances). UMAP on same data: ~5 seconds (O(N log N) ≈ 10⁴ operations). t-SNE perplexity=5 → finds tiny clusters; perplexity=50 → merges everything. UMAP n_neighbors=5 → local; n_neighbors=100 → global structure.
sections.codeBlock:
from sklearn.manifold import TSNE
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
import numpy as np

# t-SNE (slow on large data — preprocess with PCA first)
pca = PCA(n_components=50)
X_pca = pca.fit_transform(X_highdim)

tsne = TSNE(
    n_components=2,
    perplexity=30,
    learning_rate='auto',
    random_state=42
)
X_tsne = tsne.fit_transform(X_pca)

# UMAP (faster, better global structure)
# Requires: pip install umap-learn
import umap

reducer = umap.UMAP(
    n_neighbors=15,
    min_dist=0.1,
    n_components=2,
    random_state=42
)
X_umap = reducer.fit_transform(X_highdim)

# UMAP can transform new data
X_new_umap = reducer.transform(X_new)

sections.title: Anomaly Detection
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Detect points that are different from the majority. The approach depends on what "different" means: Isolated points (Isolation Forest), low-density regions (LOF), or far from normal distribution (Elliptic Envelope).

Algorithm selection:

  • Isolation Forest: Builds random trees — anomalies have shorter paths because they're easier to isolate. Works in high dimensions. Best general-purpose choice.
  • LOF (Local Outlier Factor): Compares local density of a point to its neighbors. Good for varying-density data.
  • One-Class SVM: Learns a boundary around normal data. Requires careful ν (nu) tuning. Works best after dimensionality reduction.
  • Elliptic Envelope: Assumes Gaussian distribution. Fast, but fails if data is not unimodal Gaussian.

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete: Isolation Forest on 100K points × 50D: ~50ms (fastest). LOF: ~200ms (nearest-neighbor search). One-Class SVM: ~5s (O(n²) kernel computation). At contamination=0.05 on a fraud dataset with 1% fraud, Isolation Forest catches ~80% of fraud with 3% false positive rate. LOF catches ~85% with higher FPR (~5%).
sections.codeBlock:
from sklearn.ensemble import IsolationForest
from sklearn.neighbors import LocalOutlierFactor
from sklearn.svm import OneClassSVM
from sklearn.covariance import EllipticEnvelope

# Isolation Forest — best default
model = IsolationForest(
    contamination=0.05,  # expected proportion of outliers
    random_state=42
)
labels = model.fit_predict(X)  # -1 = anomaly, 1 = normal

# LOF (local density based)
lof = LocalOutlierFactor(contamination=0.05)
labels = lof.fit_predict(X)

# One-Class SVM
ocsvm = OneClassSVM(nu=0.05, kernel='rbf', gamma='scale')
labels = ocsvm.fit_predict(X)

# Score samples by "anomaly score"
scores = model.score_samples(X)  # more negative = more anomalous
top_anomalies = np.argsort(scores)[:10]

sections.title: FAANG Interview Q&A
sections.description:
[Interview Angle] Q1: "How would you choose k in K-Means?" — Meta E4

Three methods: (1) Elbow method: plot WCSS vs k, look for the elbow point where adding more clusters gives diminishing returns. (2) Silhouette score: compute average silhouette across k values — higher is better, pick the k with max score. (3) Gap statistic: compare WCSS to a null reference distribution — pick k where gap is largest. In practice, use all three and also consider domain interpretability: does k=5 produce clusters that make business sense? For FAANG scale data (100M+ points), use a mini-batch K-Means variant and sample for k-selection.

[Interview Angle] Q2: "When would you use DBSCAN over K-Means?" — Google L4

Use DBSCAN when: (1) clusters have irregular shapes (K-Means assumes spherical), (2) clusters have varying densities, (3) you don't know the number of clusters in advance, (4) you need to identify outliers/noise explicitly, and (5) your data has non-convex clusters. A concrete example: geographic clustering of delivery locations — neighborhoods are irregular, not spherical. DBSCAN naturally finds arbitrarily shaped clusters and labels remote outliers. The downside: DBSCAN has two hyperparameters (eps, minPts) that are harder to tune than K-Means' single k.

[Interview Angle] Q3: "Explain what PCA is doing in terms of eigenvalues." — Apple E5

PCA diagonalizes the covariance matrix Σ = (1/n)XᵀX. The eigenvectors of Σ are the principal component directions; the eigenvalues λᵢ are the variances in those directions. Since Σ is real symmetric, it has orthogonal eigenvectors — the PCA rotation. The i-th eigenvalue measures how much variance is captured by PC i. Explained variance ratio = λᵢ/Σλⱼ. The SVD connection: if X = UΣVᵀ, then XᵀX = VΣ²Vᵀ. So V contains eigenvectors of XᵀX (principal components), and σᵢ² = λᵢ (singular values squared = eigenvalues). This is numerically more stable than computing the covariance matrix explicitly.

[Interview Angle] Q4: "What's the difference between t-SNE and PCA?" — Amazon L4

PCA is a linear, deterministic dimensionality reduction that preserves global variance structure. It finds orthogonal directions of maximum variance and has a closed-form solution (SVD). t-SNE is a non-linear, non-deterministic embedding that preserves local neighborhood structure. It optimizes KL divergence between pairwise similarity distributions in high-D and low-D — which makes it stochastic. PCA is great for preprocessing, denoising, and understanding global structure. t-SNE is only for visualization. Key differences: PCA has an inverse transform (can reconstruct data), t-SNE doesn't. PCA is deterministic (same result every time), t-SNE gives different results each run. PCA is fast (O(n·d)), t-SNE is O(n²).

[Interview Angle] Q5: "How would you detect anomalies in high-dimensional data?" — Google L5

High-D anomaly detection faces the curse of dimensionality — all points become equidistant, making distance-based methods (LOF, KNN) unreliable. My approach: first, reduce dimensionality using PCA or autoencoder to get a low-D representation. Then: (1) Use Isolation Forest — it doesn't rely on distances, uses decision tree paths which scale well. (2) Compute reconstruction error from an autoencoder — anomalies have high reconstruction error because they don't fit the learned manifold. (3) For feature bagging approach, train multiple detectors on random feature subsets. If the data is very high-D (10K+), consider randomized projections or UMAP for initial reduction. Always validate by inspecting the top-k anomalies and confirming they're genuinely anomalous.

sections.title: References
sections.description:
• sklearn documentation — clustering, decomposition, manifold modules
  • UMAP paper — McInnes et al. (2018): "UMAP: Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection"
  • t-SNE paper — van der Maaten & Hinton (2008): "Visualizing Data using t-SNE"
  • Outlier Analysis — Aggarwal (2017): comprehensive text on anomaly detection
  • DBSCAN paper — Ester et al. (1996): density-based clustering
  • HDBSCAN — Campello et al. (2013): hierarchical DBSCAN
